questions.question: Explain the difference between primitive variables and reference variables in Java with examples.
keyPoints: Primitive variables store actual values directly
Reference variables store memory addresses pointing to objects
Example: int x = 5; vs Dog d = new Dog();
Objects are stored in heap memory, primitives in stack

questions.question: What is aliasing in Java? Explain with a code example showing its effect.
keyPoints: Aliasing: two references pointing to same object
Code showing reference assignment
Demonstrate that modifications through one reference affect the other
Explain practical implications

questions.question: What is the difference between == operator and equals() method when comparing objects?
keyPoints: == compares references (memory addresses)
equals() compares object contents (by default inherited from Object, compares references)
String class overrides equals() to compare characters
Need to override equals() for custom object comparison

questions.question: Explain shallow copy and deep copy with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Shallow copy: copies object but not nested objects
Deep copy: copies object including all nested objects completely
Use case scenarios for each
Implementation approaches

questions.question: What is null reference in Java? What happens when you try to access members of a null reference?
keyPoints: null means reference doesn't point to any object
Default value for uninitialized reference variables
Accessing members throws NullPointerException
Can assign null to any object reference

questions.question: Explain the 'this' keyword in Java with an example.
keyPoints: 'this' refers to current object
Used to distinguish instance variables from parameters
Can be used to pass current object to methods
Example showing parameter shadowing

questions.question: Explain the concept of garbage collection in Java related to object references.
keyPoints: Objects stored in heap memory
GC reclaims memory when object has no references
Objects become eligible when reference count is zero
System.gc() suggests GC but not guaranteed

questions.question: Explain the difference between passing primitive arguments and object references to methods in Java. Also explain how Java achieves pass-by-value for objects.
keyPoints: Primitives: actual value is copied to method
Objects: reference (address) is copied, not the object
Method receives copy of reference pointing to same object
Changes to object affect original
But reassigning reference parameter doesn't affect caller
Diagram showing stack and heap during method call
Conclusion: Java is always pass-by-value, references are passed by value

questions.question: Design a Java class to demonstrate the proper implementation of deep copying using copy constructor andCloneable interface. Explain when to use each approach.
keyPoints: Explain need for deep copy
Copy constructor approach - code example
Cloneable interface - code example
Differences between approaches
When to use copy constructor vs clone
Handling nested objects in deep copy

questions.question: Write a Java program to demonstrate reference assignment, aliasing, and its effects. Include comments explaining each step and the memory changes.
keyPoints: Create class with mutable fields
Create object and reference
Assign reference to another variable
Modify through one reference
Show both see the change
Explain heap and stack representation
Show == vs equals() demonstration
Show how to create independent copy

questions.question: Explain how object references work in Java with respect to arrays and ArrayList. Write code to demonstrate array of references and how garbage collection works with arrays.
keyPoints: Array holds references to objects, not objects themselves
Array elements initialized to null by default
Array of primitives vs array of objects
Assigning objects to array elements
When array goes out of scope, objects may become eligible for GC
Code demonstration with explanations
Memory diagram showing array and objects
